summary: Gruber follows up on Mark Gurman's Bloomberg report about Apple's secret 'Marzipan' cross-platform UI project, offering his own sourced intelligence on what it actually entails. He notes that developer excitement has been based more on wishful projection than on reported technical details. His sources describe the project as a declarative control API — similar in concept to React or HTML — that would abstract differences between UIKit and AppKit. He reports the project may no longer go by 'Marzipan' internally and is almost certainly a 2019 debut, not 2018. He frames the logic as Apple simultaneously modernizing their UI paradigm and solving the cross-platform abstraction problem.
key_points: Gurman's original Marzipan report was so light on technical details that developers projected their own hopes onto it
Gruber's sources describe the project as a declarative control API, influenced by the industry trend toward declarative UI exemplified by React
The 'Marzipan' codename may only have been used in the project's earliest days — Apple insiders know it by a different, still-unleaked name
Apple is logically combining two goals: creating declarative APIs and abstracting UIKit/AppKit differences into one effort
The project is nearly certain to be a 2019 debut (macOS 10.15, iOS 13), not WWDC 2018
money_quotes: The less specific the rumor, the easier it is to project your own wishes upon it.
There are people at Apple who know about this project who first heard the name 'Marzipan' when Gurman's story was published.
Basically it sounds like a declarative control API.
In HTML you don't procedurally create elements like paragraphs, images, and tables — you declare them with tags and attributes in markup.
There's an industry-wide trend toward declaration, perhaps best exemplified by React, that could be influencing Apple in this direction.
There's nothing inherently cross-platform about a declarative control API.
If they're going to create a cross-platform UI framework, the basis for that framework should be a declarative user interface.
key_insight: Apple's cross-platform project is less about merging iOS and Mac apps and more about creating a modern declarative UI framework that naturally abstracts platform differences — an approach influenced by the broader industry shift toward declarative paradigms like React.
